title: Episode 3

jokes.text: Culminating instruction: 'Just don't... don't be yourself.' / 'I won't.'
jokes.setup: Claire gives an increasingly exhaustive list of things Fleabag shouldn't do at the party — don't talk too much, don't be funny, don't be clever, don't be the centre of attention — all circling toward the same conclusion.
jokes.punchline: 'Just don't... don't be yourself.' And Fleabag's immediate, cheerful, self-aware 'I won't.' The joke is the brutal sisterly honesty of the summation, and Fleabag's utter lack of self-pity about it.
jokes.explanation: This is an exceptional cold-open joke because it does triple duty: establishes Claire's anxiety and controlling nature, establishes their sisterly dynamic, and reveals Fleabag's resigned self-awareness without a shred of self-pity. The escalating list is classical comic structure, and 'don't be yourself' is precisely the right landing point — nothing more reductive could be said. Fleabag's instant 'I won't' is the perfect beat: agreement without hurt, which is somehow more devastating than protest. Earns its 8+ because it's character-specific, structurally elegant, and quotable.

jokes.text: 'Fuck's sake.' — Fleabag's reaction to being caught in the corridor / the building by someone who knows Claire
jokes.setup: Claire has just told Fleabag not to be seen or known, emphasising 'I know people in this building'— and immediately someone calls out 'Hi, Claire.'
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's muted 'Fuck's sake.' to camera, a perfect fourth-wall punctuation on how instantly the plan has collapsed.
jokes.explanation: The joke is entirely structural — the speed of the irony. Claire's warning is immediately made moot, and Fleabag's sotto voce 'Fuck's sake' to camera is the show's signature mode: confessional exasperation as comedy. Two words, maximum economy. The fourth-wall break makes it land specifically as Fleabag and no one else.

jokes.text: 'Put her in a quiet room with a nice breeze she'll have a panic attack... She's so happy.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag is observing Claire in crisis-management mode at the party, issuing orders about vegetarian food and waitresses.
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's aside (to camera or internally) that Claire thrives on exactly this kind of stress — 'She's so happy' — the panic attack line being the setup for that inversion.
jokes.explanation: Beautiful character observation: it's a two-beat joke where the first beat (the panic attack image) reads like sympathy or concern, then 'She's so happy' flips it into a devastating portrait of Claire's psychology. Very specific to this character and this relationship. Works as genuine comedy and as character revelation simultaneously.

jokes.text: 'Just to make sure it's not pink or anything horrifically female.'
jokes.setup: They're at a Women in Business awards ceremony, and Fleabag suggests checking the award.
jokes.punchline: The reason to check it is that pink or anything 'horrifically female' would be insulting — a dark joke about the patronising nature of women's awards, delivered pre-emptively before the show's larger satirical thread about such events.
jokes.explanation: Sharp, pointed satire that plants the episode's thematic seed — what does it mean to give women a special award for being in business? 'Horrifically female' is the right level of savage. Also a plant for the award turning out to be a sperm/tits visual gag later.

jokes.text: 'It's perfect. It looks like a sperm.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag checks the award to make sure it isn't pink or 'horrifically female' — it's fine.
jokes.punchline: Her verdict: 'It's perfect. It looks like a sperm.' The supposed neutral/masculine award looks like a sperm — itself a gendered/sexual object — which is both funnier and more ironic than if it had been pink.
jokes.explanation: This is a genuinely excellent joke. The setup from joke #4 makes it a perfect misdirection: they check to make sure it's not 'horrifically female' and the punchline is that the supposedly neutral award looks like a sperm — the most iconically male thing imaginable. It's conceptually elegant, very funny, extremely economical, and also a plant for later ('awarded her with a pair of tits'). The irony is rich: the Women in Business award that avoids femininity looks phallic. Earns its 8+ on originality and structure.

jokes.text: Claire receives an emotional 'thank you' from Fleabag for helping with the event, then immediately: 'Don't play with that.'
jokes.setup: A rare, genuine moment of gratitude between the sisters — Claire seems almost moved
jokes.punchline: Claire immediately snaps back to event management mode, killing the moment
jokes.explanation: The abrupt pivot from emotional connection to practical command is perfectly Claire. The joke lives in the timing — the audience has a half-second of warmth before the cold water. Not extraordinary but competently executed.

jokes.text: Physical comedy: Fleabag drops/nearly drops the heavy award — 'Oh, that's heavy. Argh!'
jokes.setup: Fleabag has just been told the award is worth thousands and to be careful with it.
jokes.punchline: She immediately almost drops it, crying out — the inevitable consequence of being told something is precious.
jokes.explanation: Functional physical comedy beat — very predictable (told to be careful, immediately endangers item), but executed well enough to work as a moment. The 'Argh!' is pure physical comedy from Waller-Bridge. Not particularly creative but serves the scene.

jokes.text: 'You're sweating so much.' / 'Sorry.' / 'It's attention-grabbing.' / 'It's only on one side.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag returns after breaking the award, acting like everything is fine. Claire notices she's sweating profusely.
jokes.punchline: 'It's only on one side' — Fleabag's attempt to minimize catastrophic stress-sweating by noting its asymmetry is simultaneously absurd and weirdly logical
jokes.explanation: Earns near-8 for the absurd specificity of 'only on one side' — it's not a denial, it's a bizarrely detailed qualification that reveals Fleabag has noticed and catalogued her own panic response. Character-specific: she can't lie outright so she reframes with escalating weirdness. The economy of the four-line exchange is precise.

jokes.text: 'Yeah, especially sad when you have to tell your family not to touch each other up by the photocopier.'
jokes.setup: A colleague is lamenting the corporate firings/lawsuits: 'We felt like a family.'
jokes.punchline: Fleabag can't resist adding the implied reason: the 'family' metaphor breaks down because families (ideally) don't sexually harass each other by the photocopier. It's a reference to the implied #MeToo firings, delivered as dry wit.
jokes.explanation: Sharp, dark, timely — this joke works on multiple levels: it's a character-revealing moment (Fleabag can't help herself), it's a plant for its own callback later, it punctures corporate euphemism with brutal specificity, and 'by the photocopier' is the exact right comedic detail — oddly specific and spatially embarrassing. The photocopier is funnier than 'in the office' because it's such a mundane piece of corporate furniture. A genuinely good joke.

jokes.text: 'Stop making jokes.' / 'I'm sorry. I can't help it.' / 'You can.'
jokes.setup: Claire immediately shuts down Fleabag's photocopier joke.
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's 'I can't help it' followed by Claire's flat 'You can' — Claire's certainty vs Fleabag's compulsion, with the irony that Fleabag genuinely cannot help it (it's her entire characterisation), but Claire refuses to grant her that.
jokes.explanation: A brisk character comedy beat — the two-beat exchange is perfectly timed and character-specific. Claire's refusal to admit Fleabag's compulsion is real ('You can') is both funny and a little cruel. It's the kind of moment that works better in context than it reads.

jokes.text: Belinda on courgettes: 'You can treat them appallingly and they still grow.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag offers Belinda a canapé; Belinda says she loves courgette.
jokes.punchline: Belinda's unprompted reflection on courgette: 'You can treat them appallingly and they still grow.' — delivered as wisdom, but clearly applied to something larger (women? resilience? herself?).
jokes.explanation: This is a superb introduction of Belinda as a character. The joke works as a deadpan horticultural observation (taking the vegetable comment at face value), but the subtext is unmistakably about women in business, resilience, and Belinda's own history. The line is completely straight-faced, which is what makes it funny — she means it both ways simultaneously, and doesn't wink. Character-specific: only someone like Belinda, who's been through something, would say this. Very economical.

jokes.text: Fleabag to camera after meeting Belinda: 'She seems lovely.' / Claire: 'Yeah, she's great.' — and then Claire's immediate denial of being nervous.
jokes.setup: Claire has been frantically warning Fleabag about everyone important, especially someone she's clearly anxious about.
jokes.punchline: Fleabag clocks that Claire is nervous about someone specific, Claire deflects ('I'm not nervous. I'm being completely...'), and is immediately interrupted by an arrival — the denial cut off by the very thing she was denying.
jokes.explanation: Solid structural comedy — Claire's denial is interrupted by arrival of the very person she's denying anxiety about, which is clean irony. Not remarkable but functional.

jokes.text: 'I ate a sausage over there, thinking it was a prune. Fifteen years of vegetarianism, gone. Like bang, bang.' / 'We do actually call them "bangers."' / 'Ah! That's funny! Yes, it was.'
jokes.setup: Klare (the Finnish nominee) tells Fleabag she accidentally ate a sausage — 'bang, bang' — ending 15 years of vegetarianism.
jokes.punchline: Fleabag offers the British slang: 'We do actually call them "bangers."' Klare's response — 'Ah! That's funny! Yes, it was.' — is the kicker, treating it with Finnish literal sincerity as a genuine observation rather than a joke.
jokes.explanation: The joke works on two levels: the wordplay itself (bangers/bang bang) is mild, but Klare's earnest response 'Ah! That's funny! Yes, it was.' is the real laugh — treating the pun with solemn appreciation. The comedy of a non-native speaker encountering British slang, delivered with total sincerity, is pleasantly character-specific.

jokes.text: 'Oh, just when I'm with you.' (off the wagon for champagne, specifically when with The Priest)
jokes.setup: The Priest, having just arrived, is offered champagne; someone notes he's off the wagon.
jokes.punchline: 'Oh, just when I'm with you' — implying Fleabag specifically is so destabilising that she makes a priest abandon his sobriety. It's flirtatious, confessional, and foreshadows the entire arc of their relationship.
jokes.explanation: Deceptively economical. Six words that do enormous work: they establish the priest's attraction to Fleabag, his awareness of it, his willingness to name it obliquely, and the dangerous dynamic of the whole season. The joke is romantic and funny simultaneously. 'Just when I'm with you' is a line that could be from a rom-com, except it's about abandoning sobriety, and it's said by a priest. The comedy comes from the frisson of that. Earns 8+ for character integration and economy.

jokes.text: 'I just said, "Let's go fuck like crazy tonight."'
jokes.setup: Caught muttering something inappropriate to a priest, Fleabag tries to explain what she said.
jokes.punchline: Her correction is explicitly sexual — 'Let's go fuck like crazy tonight' — which is somehow both worse than whatever she originally said and delivered with almost resigned defeat.
jokes.explanation: The escalation is the joke: when caught, her attempt at correction is explicitly sexual, which she delivers with the resigned air of someone who knows she's making it worse and can't stop herself. The Priest's 'Oh, God. Uh... OK!' reaction seals it. This is a perfect cringe-comedy beat, very Fleabag, and the delivery as a straight clarification (as if that's less bad) is structurally excellent. Earns 8+ for structure and character integration.

jokes.text: Fleabag reveals: 'His name is Klare.' Beat. 'What?' 'His name is Klare.' 'Don't.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag asks what the Finnish man's name is, implying she's about to point out something Claire is in denial about
jokes.punchline: His name is Klare — the same name as Claire. The absurdity of a Finn named Klare and the implication that Claire has fallen for someone with her own name is almost too neat to be true, which makes it funnier.
jokes.explanation: Earns 8.8 craft / 9.0 impact. This is a textbook perfect joke: the setup is innocent (just asking a name), the punchline requires a beat to land (the pause between 'His name is Klare' and 'What?' is the joke developing in real time), and the implications radiate outward — Claire has fallen for a man who is literally named after her, which means if she says 'I love Klare' she could be talking about herself. Claire's 'Don't' is the perfect button. The rhythm of the reveal is also exceptional — the second repetition forces the audience to re-hear it. This rivals the episode's best moments.

jokes.text: Fleabag's reaction beat to 'Klare' — the beat before she says 'Don't.' [Claire's pre-emption]
jokes.setup: Fleabag has just learned the man's name is Klare.
jokes.punchline: Claire's instant 'Don't.' — shutting down Fleabag's reaction before it happens — is itself a joke, because it confirms Claire knows exactly how absurd this is and is pre-emptively defensive.
jokes.explanation: The button on the name joke. Claire's 'Don't.' is perfectly timed — it comes before Fleabag's reaction materialises, which means Claire is so aware of the absurdity that she's already defending against mockery. The pre-emption is funnier than any actual joke Fleabag could make. This is the second half of one of the episode's best two-beat jokes.

jokes.text: Fleabag announces 'Klare Korhonen' as a nominee, apparently accidentally — 'Oh, uh, sorry. I-I think there's been a mistake here.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag is reading the nominees from a card; she encounters the name 'Klare' — the same name as Claire — and her brain short-circuits.
jokes.punchline: She reads it out, then immediately realises what she's done and tries to walk it back while Klare is standing right there. The awkward 'sorry' and attempted correction is visible cringe.
jokes.explanation: The callback to the name joke works because the audience now understands why Fleabag momentarily chokes — she's seeing Klare's name and it's triggering her knowledge of Claire's feelings. The public stumble is funnier because of that private context.

jokes.text: 'It has to go like "cockwork."' / 'Like what?' / '"Cockwork."' / 'Claire, your brain is somewhere else right now.'
jokes.setup: Claire is giving Fleabag precise instructions about the ceremony — it has to run perfectly.
jokes.punchline: She says 'cockwork' instead of 'clockwork' — a Freudian slip that exposes exactly where her mind is (on Klare). Fleabag's dry diagnosis — 'Claire, your brain is somewhere else right now' — is the button.
jokes.explanation: A Freudian slip so perfectly placed it feels almost too good, but it works. The slip is character-specific (Claire's suppressed attraction to Klare/men named Klare), structurally excellent (the callback is earned and the line is quotable), and Fleabag's straight-faced observation 'your brain is somewhere else right now' is the ideal response. 'Cockwork' is funny on its own; in this context it's a minor gem. Earns 8+ for character integration and economy.

jokes.text: Fleabag steps up to replace the apparently incapacitated Sylvia: 'Um, I'm sorry. It appears that Sylvia is busy, which shouldn't come as a surprise, really.'
jokes.setup: Sylvia was supposed to present the award but appears to be down after her entrance disaster.
jokes.punchline: 'It appears that Sylvia is busy, which shouldn't come as a surprise, really' — a magnificently understated and slightly brutal deflection. The 'shouldn't come as a surprise' is perfectly calibrated ambiguity: is Sylvia known for being unreliable? Drunk? Injured?
jokes.explanation: Excellent ad-lib-style joke delivered as if it's the most natural thing in the world. The comedy is in the 'shouldn't come as a surprise, really' — it implies this is Sylvia's pattern, which we know nothing about and are left to imagine. Deadpan understatement at its finest. The structure of 'explains a crisis while appearing to minimize it' is classic.

jokes.text: 'It's been a big year for business. Particularly women in business. Men have been pretty hands-on the past few decades.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag is vamping at the microphone, filling time with observations about 'business.'
jokes.punchline: 'Men have been pretty hands-on the past few decades' — a devastatingly understated reference to sexual harassment/assault in the workplace, delivered as if it's a neutral observation.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. 'Hands-on' is doing extraordinary double work — it's a legitimate business buzzword (engaged, involved) and a precise euphemism for sexual harassment. The fact that it's delivered as a completely straight business observation makes it funnier and sharper. Maximum economy: four words ('pretty hands-on the past') contain the entire joke. Contextually brave (2019 BBC, post-#MeToo). Earns 8+ for originality, economy, and earned wit.

jokes.text: '...family really shouldn't touch each other up next to the photocopier.' (repeated as formal speech)
jokes.setup: Fleabag's earlier joke at the party about 'family' and photocopier groping was a throwaway; now she's repeating it in a formal speech at the microphone.
jokes.punchline: Hearing the same joke delivered as official company remarks — with the structure 'if we've learned anything this year' — is funnier and darker. The audience at the event laughs ('Funny!'), which is both a gag and a meta-comment.
jokes.explanation: The callback elevates the original joke significantly. When a private throwaway becomes formal corporate remarks, it gains a whole new dimension: it's no longer just Fleabag being unable to help herself, it's Fleabag accidentally giving a genuinely trenchant speech about workplace harassment. The in-show audience laughing ('Funny!') is a meta-joke about how the truth can be so stark it becomes comedy. The structure of callback-as-escalation is excellent.

jokes.text: In-room reaction: 'Funny!' — someone in the audience responds to Fleabag's photocopier line
jokes.setup: Fleabag has just delivered the photocopier line in a formal speech.
jokes.punchline: Someone in the room says 'Funny!' — as if officially approving the joke. It's meta because the audience at home is also laughing, and having the in-show audience validate it is both slightly surreal and a comment on how dark truths can be applauded as entertainment.
jokes.explanation: The 'Funny!' punctuation works as a micro-joke — the formality of someone declaring a joke funny is itself slightly absurd. It also validates Fleabag's instinct from joke #9 ('I can't help it') — she was right, she IS funny, and now a room of business people is applauding her for it.

jokes.text: 'Well, I was going to say this is a bit on the nose, but she doesn't seem to have one.'
jokes.setup: Belinda has won the Women in Business award, which turns out to look explicitly like a pair of breasts (not a sperm after all — or perhaps it's been dropped/damaged and now looks different). Claire is horrified.
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's quip: 'a bit on the nose, but she doesn't seem to have one' — referring to the anatomy of the sculpture, which apparently lacks a nose. It's a pun on 'on the nose' (obvious/blunt) while also being a literal observation about the sculpture's anatomy.
jokes.explanation: A genuinely clever pun that works on two levels simultaneously: idiomatic ('on the nose' = obvious) and literal (the sculpture literally doesn't have a nose). The setup from joke #5 (it looks like a sperm) makes the payoff that it now looks like breasts/tits an escalation. The line is delivered as an aside, which is the right choice. Compact, double-meaning, earns its 8.

jokes.text: 'It's not fine. I awarded her with a pair of tits.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag says 'It'll be fine' about the award situation.
jokes.punchline: Claire's clarification: 'I awarded her with a pair of tits.' The specificity ('a pair of tits') is funny — it's the precise, clinical, mortified summary of what has happened.
jokes.explanation: The payoff on the award's visual gag. 'I awarded her with a pair of tits' is both the literal situation and an ironic completion of the 'horrifically female' setup — they checked it wasn't pink/female, and somehow it turned into tits anyway. Claire's mortification expressed with such blunt precision is character-specific. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Claire ordering Fleabag to 'Chase her down' and Fleabag questioning this — 'Chase her down?' / 'It's a stolen piece of art.'
jokes.setup: The award has been presented to Belinda, who now possesses a piece of stolen art.
jokes.punchline: Claire's instruction — 'Chase her down' — applied to a dignified CEO at a formal awards ceremony, followed by the explanation 'It's a stolen piece of art,' which is both completely true and sounds unhinged in this context.
jokes.explanation: Good escalation — 'chase her down' at a formal event is absurd, and the justification 'it's a stolen piece of art' makes perfect logical sense while being completely chaotic. The exchange has good rhythm.

jokes.text: Belinda in the corridor: 'I'm trained in martial arts. It's just the basics, but it is enough.' — said to Fleabag who is apparently trying to retrieve the award
jokes.setup: Fleabag has been sent to 'chase down' Belinda and retrieve the award; she encounters Belinda in what is presumably a stairwell or corridor.
jokes.punchline: Belinda's perfectly calm disclosure: 'I'm trained in martial arts. It's just the basics, but it is enough.' — a pre-emptive warning delivered as casual information, not a threat. The 'it is enough' is the kicker.
jokes.explanation: This is a superb character introduction line. The joke has three beats: (1) the unexpected disclosure (martial arts), (2) the immediate qualifier ('just the basics'), which creates a beat of almost seeming modest, and (3) the flip ('but it is enough') which restores the warning. The qualificationremoval structure is brilliant. It's entirely specific to Belinda as a character — a woman who has been 'hands-on'd' by decades of corporate culture and is quietly lethal. Very quotable and rewatch-worthy. Earns 8+ easily.

jokes.text: 'Oh, it's infantilising bollocks.' / 'Well, don't you think it's good that...' / 'No. No. It's ghettoising. It's a subsection of success.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag congratulates Belinda; Belinda has just won 'Best Woman in Business.'
jokes.punchline: Belinda's verdict on her own award: 'infantilising bollocks.' When Fleabag tries to find the positive ('don't you think it's good that...'), Belinda cuts her off: 'No. No. It's ghettoising. It's a subsection of success.'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant. 'A subsection of success' is exactly the right articulation, delivered with complete conviction by someone who just received the award and knows its limits. The episode has been building toward this critique — from Fleabag's 'horrifically female' worry to the tits-shaped award — and Belinda's dismissal is its thesis statement. The comedy is that she won it and immediately eviscerates it. The line 'it's ghettoising' has the force of someone who has thought about this for thirty years.

jokes.text: 'Ah, it's the fucking children's table of awards.'
jokes.setup: Continuation of Belinda's assessment of women's awards.
jokes.punchline: 'It's the fucking children's table of awards' — the metaphor is perfect. It escalates 'subsection of success' into something vivid and immediately legible.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines, period. 'The fucking children's table of awards' is a perfect metaphor — specific, vivid, immediately understood, and completely devastating. It takes the abstract critique ('ghettoising') and gives it a domestic, humiliating image. The 'fucking' is essential — it's the controlled profanity of someone who has been politely tolerating this for decades and has finally stopped. Extremely quotable, very high impact. Earns its 8+ without question.

jokes.text: Belinda's reason for attending despite hating the award: 'Because I'd be an arsehole not to.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag asks why Belinda went to the award ceremony if she thinks it's infantilising bollocks.
jokes.punchline: 'Because I'd be an arsehole not to.' — the entire contradiction of professional life for accomplished women summed up in eight words.
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy. The joke is the trapped logic: the award is infantilising, but refusing it is its own kind of punishment (ingratitude, arrogance). Belinda has no good option and knows it. Eight words capture the double-bind of professional women navigating institutional recognition they didn't ask for. Deeply character-specific and earns its 8+ for economy and character integration.

jokes.text: Belinda's favourite 'period film' is Carrie.
jokes.setup: Belinda has asked Fleabag if she likes old films; they're bonding. 'And what's your favourite period film?'
jokes.punchline: 'Carrie.' — a film notorious for its period blood imagery, telekinetic revenge, and prom carnage. A 'period film' that is not a period drama but is entirely about periods, in the most violent possible way.
jokes.explanation: This is a brilliant joke, possibly the episode's single best line for sheer elegance. The setup 'what's your favourite period film?' is completely innocent — it's a normal question about costume dramas. The punchline 'Carrie' works on three levels: (1) wordplay (period/periods), (2) character revelation (Belinda is a woman who channels women's rage and finds catharsis in Carrie's telekinetic revenge), and (3) the sheer comedy of the non-sequitur landing like a perfect answer. It also prefigures Belinda's entire speech about women being born with pain built in — Carrie is literally about that. One word, maximum impact, completely specific to this character. This is among the best jokes in the series.

jokes.text: Fleabag's reaction to 'Carrie' — 'God.' followed by 'God, you are a tonic.'
jokes.setup: Belinda delivers 'Carrie.'
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's response: first a simple 'God.' (pure reaction, possibly to camera), then 'God, you are a tonic.' — which is both how she feels and a funny word for 'you are amazing' said with genuine delight.
jokes.explanation: The reaction beat earns most of its score from riding on the brilliance of joke #33. 'God, you are a tonic' is a lovely bit of period-accurate (in feel) British vocabulary that also sets up the conversation about Fleabag needing exactly this kind of mentor figure.

jokes.text: Fleabag, asked if she's a 'woman in business': 'I run a café. / Oh, good for you. / Did you make the canapés? / Uh, no, actually, I stole them.'
jokes.setup: Belinda asks what Fleabag does; 'I run a café' is a modest answer at a high-powered event.
jokes.punchline: Belinda's slightly patronising 'Oh, good for you' is one comic beat; then her follow-up 'Did you make the canapés?' (connecting the café to catering), and Fleabag's perfectly straight 'Uh, no, actually, I stole them.'
jokes.explanation: Multi-beat joke with a great landing. The slight condescension of 'Oh, good for you' is funny (Belinda briefly lapses into the exact patronising attitude she scorns), and then Fleabag's casual 'I stole them' is a perfect deadpan truth-bomb. It's characterful, honest, and the complete non-explanation is better than any explanation would be. Earns 8 for structure and character integration.

jokes.text: Belinda's speech: 'Women are born with pain built in... Men don't. They have to seek it out. They invent all these gods and demons... so they can feel guilty about things, which is something we do very well on our own. And then they create wars so they can feel things and touch each other, and when there aren't any wars, they can play rugby.'
jokes.setup: Belinda is delivering an impromptu, slightly drunk speech about the difference between men and women's relationship with pain.
jokes.punchline: 'And when there aren't any wars, they can play rugby.' — the escalation from gods/guilt/wars to rugby as the comedic deflation is perfect. It's a joke buried in a serious speech.
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional writing. The speech is primarily dramatic/thematic, but it's also genuinely funny — the logic escalates from cosmic (gods, demons, guilt) to historical (wars) to mundane (rugby), and that deflation is the joke. The absurdity is that the logic is flawless: men did invent religions partly as guilt-frameworks, and they do play rugby. The observation about men creating wars 'so they can touch each other' is sharply funny and subversive. Earns 9+ for originality, character integration, and the way it embeds comedy within genuine philosophy.

jokes.text: Belinda on the menopause: '...the most wonderful fucking thing in the world. And yes, your entire pelvic floor crumbles and you get fucking hot, and no one cares, but then... you're free.'
jokes.setup: Belinda has built to 'And then, just when you feel you are making peace with it all — the menopause comes.'
jokes.punchline: The misdirect: 'the menopause comes, and it is... the most wonderful fucking thing in the world.' The audience (and Fleabag) expects horror; Belinda flips to triumph. Then the qualifications ('your entire pelvic floor crumbles') make the triumph even funnier and more real.
jokes.explanation: Structurally brilliant misdirection. The entire speech has been building dread — pain, cycles, decades of suffering — and 'the menopause comes' is the announced climax of that dread. The flip to 'the most wonderful fucking thing in the world' is a genuine surprise that recontextualises everything. Then 'yes, your entire pelvic floor crumbles' both validates the dread and refuses to undercut the triumph. The comedy and the philosophy are inseparable. Earns 9+ for structure and character integration.

jokes.text: 'It is not a party until someone flirts with you.'
jokes.setup: Belinda is sending Fleabag back to the party.
jokes.punchline: This is Belinda's personal definition of a party — not good food or drink, but someone flirting with you. It's a joke and also an expression of character.
jokes.explanation: A good Belinda line — her definition of life's pleasures is specific and characterful. Sets up her monologue about flirtation that follows.

jokes.text: Belinda: '...grab the night by its nipples and go and flirt with someone.' / Fleabag: 'Mm. No, that's not what I meant.'
jokes.setup: Belinda is urging Fleabag to flirt and seize the night.
jokes.punchline: 'Grab the night by its nipples' — an absurdist riff on 'grab life by the balls' that's characteristically Belinda: the body part is different, slightly surreal, and yet somehow exactly right for this woman's worldview. Fleabag's 'that's not what I meant' is the straight-man button.
jokes.explanation: Highly quotable and characterful. 'Grab the night by its nipples' is a perfect Belinda coinage — it's the female equivalent of 'grab life by the balls,' except it's stranger, more specific, and more interesting. The word 'nipples' is funny in context (it's not crude, it's surreal) and it captures Belinda's entire ethos: the same life-force energy as the male idiom, but routed through female anatomy. Fleabag's flat 'that's not what I meant' as immediate response is the right comedy beat. Earns 8+ for originality, economy, and character integration.

jokes.text: Belinda: 'Look at me. Listen. People are all we've got. People are all we've got, so grab the night by its nipples and go and flirt with someone.'
jokes.setup: Belinda has just conceded that most people are shit, but recovers to give Fleabag motivational advice ending with a stirring call to action
jokes.punchline: 'Grab the night by its nipples' — delivered as if it's a standard inspirational phrase. The specificity of 'nipples' where any other body part would be less funny makes this quotable.
jokes.explanation: Earns 8.7 craft / 9.0 impact. 'Grab the night by its nipples' is the kind of phrase that becomes a cultural reference because it has no precedent — it's not a variation on 'grab life by the horns,' it's its own thing. The context makes it perfect: a 58-year-old who has survived everything, delivering a mantra to a younger woman. The nipples image connects to the menopause speech (the body, female experience) while also being completely deranged as a pep talk. Highly quotable, completely original.

jokes.text: Belinda: 'Oh. I wish you were my type.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag has said 'I want to do it with you' (following up the flirting discussion).
jokes.punchline: Belinda's 'I wish you were my type' — a graceful, funny, and not-unkind rejection that simultaneously reveals Belinda's sexuality and pays Fleabag a compliment.
jokes.explanation: A lovely, economical character moment. 'I wish you were my type' is both funny and moving — it's a rejection that's also an affirmation. The comedy is in the gentle surprise of it and in Belinda's wistfulness.

jokes.text: Belinda: 'I can't be arsed, darling.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag has asked why Belinda won't — 'Why not? / Honestly?'
jokes.punchline: 'I can't be arsed, darling.' — the most wonderfully direct possible response to a sexual advance, delivered with complete equanimity and a 'darling' that makes it even funnier.
jokes.explanation: Belinda's most Belinda line. She has just given a speech about women's liberation and freedom from being 'a machine with parts' — and her practical application of that freedom is 'I can't be arsed.' It's a character-perfect punchline. The 'darling' is essential — it softens nothing while also being warm. Very quotable. Earns 8+ for character integration, economy, and for being the joke that proves Belinda means what she says.

jokes.text: 'And what had Jesus done by 33? / Died? / Exactly. So get out there and flirt.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag says '33 isn't exactly...' (as if 33 is too young to be inspiring). Belinda invokes Jesus.
jokes.punchline: Fleabag: 'Died?' — the genuine answer to 'what had Jesus done by 33' in terms of most memorable things. Belinda's 'Exactly. So get out there and flirt.' takes Fleabag's joke seriously and uses it as motivation.
jokes.explanation: A perfect three-beat exchange. The Jesus invocation is unexpected in this context; Fleabag's 'Died?' is technically correct and darkly funny; Belinda's 'Exactly' treats the death as motivational rather than deflating, which is the real joke. It's also quietly resonant given the show's season-long engagement with faith (The Priest) and that Fleabag is 33 — the same age Jesus died. The line 'so get out there and flirt' as the logical conclusion of Jesus's death is absurdist and perfect.

jokes.text: Fleabag returns; Claire: 'Thought you might be snogging Finland.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag returns to the party after her corridor conversation with Belinda; Claire checks in.
jokes.punchline: 'Thought you might be snogging Finland' — using the country as a nickname for Klare is both funny (reductive, a bit dismissive) and reveals that Claire has been watching/worrying even while managing the event.
jokes.explanation: 'Snogging Finland' is a funny bit of casual xenonym usage — she means Klare but says 'Finland' as if that's sufficient identification. It's funny and character-specific: Claire would reduce a person she's trying not to care about to a country.

jokes.text: Claire's eruption: 'You just think you can do whatever you like, say whatever you like, steal whatever you like, kiss whoever you like.' / 'He kissed me!' / 'Oh, I know!' / 'You know?'
jokes.setup: Claire has been building toward confrontation; she lists Fleabag's transgressions in an escalating rant.
jokes.punchline: The triple rhythm of 'whatever you like' suddenly includes 'kiss whoever you like' — which Fleabag contradicts ('He kissed me!'), and Claire's immediate 'Oh, I know!' followed by Fleabag's shocked 'You know?' reveals Claire was aware the whole time.
jokes.explanation: The escalating 'whatever you like' list builds to a funny climax, and then the revelation that Claire already knew about the kiss adds a twist: she's not furious about the kiss specifically, she's furious about something else. The comedy and the drama fuse perfectly. 'He kissed me!' / 'Oh, I know!' is a great two-line exchange.

jokes.text: '...with your quirky café and your dead best friend.'
jokes.setup: Claire is telling Fleabag that she'll always be fine and interesting.
jokes.punchline: 'Your quirky café and your dead best friend' — Claire weaponises Fleabag's grief and her defining characteristics against her in the same breath, in a way that's genuinely cruel and also shockingly funny.
jokes.explanation: Devastating. 'Your dead best friend' is the most brutal possible thing Claire could say — she's reducing Boo's death to Fleabag's personality accessory, her source of 'interesting.' The comedy and the horror are completely fused. It's character-specific to Claire (her pain coming out as cruelty) and to Fleabag (who uses her grief in exactly the way Claire is accusing). One of the episode's most memorable moments. Earns 8+ for character integration, earned darkness, and the shock of the specificity.

jokes.text: Claire: 'If you mention the size of my office, I will scream.' / Fleabag: 'It's huge.'
jokes.setup: The evening has been emotionally devastating; they're in Claire's enormous office.
jokes.punchline: After everything, Fleabag can't resist: 'It's huge.' The beat after Claire's threat makes it even better — Fleabag waits the minimum amount of time before doing exactly what she was warned not to do.
jokes.explanation: A perfect callback that pays off two things at once: the office's size (mentioned from the beginning) and the instruction from joke #1 ('just don't be yourself'). After all the emotional intensity of their fight, Fleabag being herself — helplessly, immediately — is the most honest thing. Two words. Perfect. The setup ('I will scream') and the instant, unrepentant 'It's huge' is a textbook comedy beat. Earns 8+ for structure, economy, and character integration.

jokes.text: 'We're not friends. We are sisters. Get your own friends.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag says 'I just thought we were hanging out. Just as friends.'
jokes.punchline: Claire: 'We're not friends. We are sisters. Get your own friends.' — the distinction is both true and devastating, delivered with complete Claire-ish flatness.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most quoted lines. The joke is in the logic: sisterhood is obligatory love, friendship is chosen love. Claire is clarifying that she has no choice about Fleabag — they're sisters — and therefore what Fleabag is interpreting as warmth is actually just biological fate. 'Get your own friends' is the kicker: practical, slightly cruel, completely Claire. It's a devastating articulation of a certain kind of sibling relationship. Earns 8+ for character integration, economy, and the fact that it's genuinely memorable.

jokes.text: The Priest: 'This is a bit embarrassing, but recently I've been really enjoying going to bed at 9:30.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag has shown up at The Priest's door late at night for company.
jokes.punchline: His slightly embarrassed admission is that he's become a 9:30 bedtime person — which is both a funny priest-specific detail and also the comedy of a young-ish man having to confess to a genuinely boring life habit.
jokes.explanation: A nice, gentle joke — the 'embarrassing' framing makes it funnier (he thinks this is something to be embarrassed about). It establishes the priest's domesticity and sets up Pam.

jokes.text: Fleabag: 'I just fancied a drink and a priest and a chat, maybe.' / Priest: 'Oh, that's my whole job.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag explains why she's shown up.
jokes.punchline: 'That's my whole job.' — The Priest's dry self-description is both funny and a tiny bit sad. 'A drink and a priest and a chat' is a funny way to describe his entire vocation.
jokes.explanation: Economical and warm. 'That's my whole job' is The Priest's character in four words: he takes his calling seriously, finds it genuinely fulfilling, and is self-deprecatingly aware of how simple it sounds. Good setup/punchline structure.

jokes.text: 'We'll have to be quiet though because Pam's a bit of a sound tyrant in the evenings.' / 'Pam lives here?'
jokes.setup: The Priest invites Fleabag in.
jokes.punchline: 'Pam's a bit of a sound tyrant' — a characterful description of a housemate/parishioner; then Fleabag's 'Pam lives here?' which reveals this is an unexpected domestic detail about a priest's life.
jokes.explanation: 'Sound tyrant' is a funny compound — it's specific and absurd in a small-scale domestic way. The surprise that Pam lives with the priest is a good comic beat for the relationship (he has far less romantic freedom/privacy than implied).

jokes.text: Fleabag identifies 'inconsistencies' in the Bible: 'The world was made in seven days... light came... and then a few days later, the sun came.' / Priest: 'Yeah, that's ridiculous.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag has read The Priest's book (the Bible) and found 'one or two little inconsistencies.'
jokes.punchline: The Priest's immediate, undefended 'Yeah, that's ridiculous' — agreeing with the inconsistency instead of defending it — is the joke. He doesn't even try to argue.
jokes.explanation: The joke is The Priest's instantaneous capitulation — 'Yeah, that's ridiculous' — which both characterises him as intellectually honest and sets up his faith as something more than literal belief. His failure to defend Genesis is funnier than any defence would be.

jokes.text: Fleabag: 'But you believe that.' / Priest: 'But it's not fact. It's poetry, it's moral code. It's for interpretation...'
jokes.setup: Fleabag challenges The Priest on believing something he's admitted is 'ridiculous.'
jokes.punchline: His explanation ('it's poetry, it's moral code') is internally coherent but also slightly absurd as a defence of literal belief — delivered with the patient air of someone who has had this conversation many times.
jokes.explanation: A functional comedy beat that does more dramatic work than comedy work — it establishes his theology while also being slightly funny that 'it's not fact, it's poetry' is the entire defence. The patient delivery is character-specific.

jokes.text: 'We can arrange that.' / 'A father of many.' / 'I'll go up to three.' / 'It's not gonna happen.' / 'Two, then.' / 'OK, two.'
jokes.setup: The Priest says God meant for him 'to love people as a father.' Fleabag immediately hears an invitation.
jokes.punchline: Fleabag starts negotiating the number of children they'll have — 'I'll go up to three' — and The Priest weakens: 'OK, two.' The priest entering a negotiation about how many children they'll have together is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Excellent comic escalation. The joke works because The Priest enters the negotiation — 'OK, two' — which means he's been baited into treating an absurd premise seriously. Fleabag's negotiating tactics (start at three, settle at two) are both funny and flirtatious. The exchange reveals their chemistry perfectly: she pushes, he resists, then gives a little. Earns 8+ for structure, character integration, and the sheer comedy of a priest agreeing to two children.

jokes.text: 'I like that you believe in a meaningless existence.'
jokes.setup: The Priest is explaining why Fleabag shouldn't convert.
jokes.punchline: 'I like that you believe in a meaningless existence' — an extraordinary thing for a priest to say, and it's delivered as a genuine compliment.
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in the inversion: a priest should want people to find meaning through God, but he finds Fleabag's atheism/nihilism admirable and wants to preserve it. It's charming, slightly absurd, and deeply character-specific. Also foreshadows his later statement that she makes him question his faith. Earns 8+ for originality and character integration.

jokes.text: The Priest: 'No, I'm not being funny, foxes have been after me for years. It's like they have a pact or something.'
jokes.setup: They've heard something in the garden; The Priest thinks it's a fox.
jokes.punchline: The Priest's revelation that he has a fox problem — not a normal one, but a personal vendetta: 'foxes have been after me for years. It's like they have a pact or something.'
jokes.explanation: An unexpected character revelation that's endearingly absurd. A man of God who has a persecution complex about foxes — specifically, who believes foxes have organised against him — is funny and oddly characterful. It humanises him in a slightly deranged direction.

jokes.text: The Priest's train toilet anecdote: '...a toilet of a train, and when the train stopped, a fucking fox tried to get through the window. Of a train! Its face was in the window.'
jokes.setup: The Priest is itemising evidence for the fox conspiracy.
jokes.punchline: The train toilet story — a fox trying to get through the window of a moving-train toilet — is the kind of specific, absurd detail that feels true precisely because no one would invent it. 'Of a train!' as a self-interrupting clarification is the comedic peak.
jokes.explanation: Highly specific absurdism at its best. 'Of a train!' as an unnecessary clarification (as if there are many kinds of train toilet windows being targeted) is perfect comic rhythm — he's so committed to the specificity that he interrupts himself. The image of a fox face in a train toilet window is vividly funny. The detail 'its face was in the window' — not 'it was at the window' — suggests intimate, intentional contact. Earns 8+ for originality and the precision of the detail.

jokes.text: The fox monastery story: 'And once, when I was at a monastery, I woke up just feeling a bit weird — like there might be a fox about — and a fox was sitting underneath my window, looking at me like this. Pointing at me like, "You... we're watching you. We're having you."'
jokes.setup: The Priest escalates from the train toilet to a monastery encounter.
jokes.punchline: The fox was 'pointing at' him and saying (in The Priest's imagined fox-voice) 'We're having you.' — the escalation from observation to threat to the comedy of him giving the fox a voice and a plan.
jokes.explanation: The monastery story escalates perfectly. The comedy is that he was at a monastery — a place of spiritual safety — and was still being surveilled by foxes. His ventriloquism of the fox ('We're watching you. We're having you.') is committed, slightly unhinged, and completely character-specific. A priest who has been stalked by foxes from train toilets to monasteries and knows their voice — this is original character comedy.

jokes.text: Fleabag: 'Lucky God got there first.' / Priest: 'Well, yeah.' / Fleabag: 'You could be a fox boy by now.' / Priest: 'And we all know what happened to them.'
jokes.setup: The Priest has described the fox's claim: 'We're having you.'
jokes.punchline: 'Lucky God got there first' / 'You could be a fox boy by now' — Fleabag treats the fox conspiracy with complete narrative seriousness, imagining an alternative fate. Then The Priest's 'And we all know what happened to them' — implying some dark fate for fox boys — is the perfect absurdist button.
jokes.explanation: Brilliant exchange. Fleabag treating the fox conspiracy as a legitimate alternative spiritual path ('you could be a fox boy by now') is perfect absurdist escalation, and The Priest's 'We all know what happened to them' — implying a specific, terrible fate for fox-claimed humans — commits to the mythology with complete seriousness. It's funny because they're both in the bit together. The faith/fox parallel (God vs fox as rival claimants for his soul) is also quietly thematic.

jokes.text: Fleabag interrupts herself mid-word with 'Oh, it's a fucking fox!'
jokes.setup: Fleabag is in the middle of raising the delicate subject of celibacy — 'Especially as a...' — a serious, charged moment.
jokes.punchline: She is interrupted by an actual fox. The thing The Priest has been warning about for two minutes materialises at the worst possible comedic moment, derailing the entire emotional conversation.
jokes.explanation: The perfect payoff on the fox conspiracy thread. The comedy is entirely structural: the fox appears at the exact moment the scene reaches maximum emotional tension (she's about to say 'celibacy'). The intrusion is physically and emotionally absurd, and it validates The Priest's entire persecution narrative. The setup took three minutes; the payoff is four words. Excellent structural comedy.

jokes.text: Priest: 'Chill out about the fox.' / Fleabag: 'Oh, sorry. I just don't know what they want from me.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag has reacted dramatically to the fox.
jokes.punchline: Now The Priest is the calm one — 'Chill out about the fox' — while Fleabag has adopted his earlier panic. And 'I just don't know what they want from me' mirrors his earlier fear, flipped: now she's the one with the existential fox anxiety.
jokes.explanation: A role-reversal callback that works beautifully. The Priest built the fox mythology; now Fleabag has caught the anxiety. 'I just don't know what they want from me' is her adopting his exact ontological uncertainty about foxes, and it's funnier because she said it only seconds after meeting one. Perfect comic inversion.

jokes.text: The Priest: 'Celibacy is a lot less complicated than... romantic relationships.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag has said she couldn't give up sex forever — it's 'too... too...' — and The Priest fills in the blank.
jokes.punchline: 'Celibacy is a lot less complicated than... romantic relationships' — the beat between 'than' and 'romantic relationships' is the joke: he started to say something more specific ('than what I feel for you') and caught himself, retreating to generality.
jokes.explanation: This is a masterclass in dramatic comedy. The pause mid-sentence — 'a lot less complicated than...' — does everything: it reveals what he was about to say, shows him stopping himself, and the landing ('romantic relationships') is both technically accurate and obviously a euphemism for 'you.' The joke is entirely in the gap between what's started and what's completed, and it mirrors Fleabag's own unfinished sentences throughout. Perfect structure. Earns 9+ for character integration and structural sophistication.

jokes.text: The Priest: 'We're not gonna have sex.' — delivered quietly, directly, unprompted
jokes.setup: The conversation about celibacy and romantic relationships is building toward something unspoken.
jokes.punchline: He simply states it, quietly, 'We're not gonna have sex' — before she's asked, before anything more has been said. The preemptive declaration is both funny (the absurdity of saying it unprompted) and devastating (because it means he's thinking about it constantly).
jokes.explanation: One of the series' most iconic lines. The comedy is entirely in the structure: it's unprompted, delivered with quiet certainty, and the very fact of saying it reveals everything. If he didn't want to have sex with her, he wouldn't need to say it. The declaration IS the confession. It's funny and heartbreaking in equal measure. Five words, maximum impact. This is the kind of line that makes a season. Earns 9+ on all fronts.

jokes.text: Fleabag: 'I know that's what you think you want from me, but it's not.' / Priest: 'Well, it might.' / Fleabag: 'It won't.' / Priest: 'I've been there many times... before I found this.' / Fleabag: 'How many times?'
jokes.setup: The Priest has said they won't have sex; he says he knows what she wants and it won't bring good.
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's 'Well, it might.' — arguing with a priest about whether sex would bring good. And then 'How many times?' when he says he's been there 'many, many times' — her immediate interest in his sexual history.
jokes.explanation: The exchange reveals so much while being genuinely funny. 'Well, it might' is Fleabag at her most irrepressibly herself — she cannot help arguing for the possibility of good sex even with a priest who has just stated clearly they won't. And 'How many times?' — when he says 'many, many times' about his past — is pure Fleabag: the immediate tally-keeping of someone very interested. Character-perfect.

jokes.text: Priest: 'I know that's what you think you want from me, but it's not.'
jokes.setup: Following 'We're not gonna have sex,' Fleabag hasn't denied it
jokes.punchline: His '...but it's not' — asserting he knows what she wants better than she does — is both presumptuous and possibly correct. It's funny because it's a priest telling someone their desires are wrong, which is on-brand, but also because he might be right.
jokes.explanation: Follows naturally from the previous joke and maintains the tone. His certainty about her desires is funny in context — he's a priest making sexual pronouncements while clearly having the feelings he's describing.

jokes.text: 'I've been there many times... before I found this. Many, many times. / How many times?'
jokes.setup: The Priest is speaking gravely about his past — implying extensive romantic/sexual history before his vocation
jokes.punchline: Fleabag: 'How many times?' — the question is completely off the spiritual point but exactly the right question for a curious, attracted person. His drawn-out 'Many.' after a long pause is funnier than any number.
jokes.explanation: Earns 8.4 for the perfect timing of the question vs. the pause before 'Many.' Fleabag's question is both the audience's question (we also want to know) and a character-specific intrusion of the personal into the spiritual. The Priest's pause before 'Many' is worth more than any specific number — it implies a past so extensive he's still counting. The restraint of 'Many' as the complete answer is impeccable.

jokes.text: Priest: 'I'd really like to be your friend, though.' / Fleabag: 'I'd like to be your friend, too.' / 'We'll last a week.'
jokes.setup: After all the charged conversation about sex and celibacy, they agree to friendship.
jokes.punchline: 'We'll last a week.' — which character says this isn't entirely clear, but it's Fleabag's voice: absolute certainty that the friendship will last one week before something happens.
jokes.explanation: An excellent, economical button on the entire scene. After all the charged denial, 'We'll last a week' is both funny (the specific time frame is perfect — not 'a day,' not 'a month,' but one week) and prescient. It's also honest: neither of them actually believes the friendship will be platonic. Earns 8 for character integration and economy.

jokes.text: The Priest: 'Where'd you just go? You just... went somewhere.' — noticing Fleabag's camera-break / fourth-wall moment
jokes.setup: Fleabag has had a private moment — looking to camera, sharing something with the audience — mid-conversation.
jokes.punchline: The Priest notices. 'Where'd you just go? There. There.' — he can SEE her looking away to the invisible camera, and he's calling it out. This is the show's central meta-joke: the fourth wall exists, and a character is now observing it.
jokes.explanation: This is arguably the episode's — and the season's — most significant comedic and dramatic moment. The show's central formal device (Fleabag's direct address to camera/audience) is here made diegetic: The Priest can see it. He doesn't know what it is, but he notices when she 'goes somewhere.' The comedy is in the impossibility of the moment (how can he see the camera-break?) and in Fleabag's response ('Nowhere') — she lies, which is funny and devastating. This is a truly original, structurally brilliant meta-joke that no other show has done. It works as a joke, as a romantic beat, and as a philosophical statement about intimacy. The fact that he can SEE her private world means the fourth wall is being broken from both sides. This scores at the very top of the scale.

jokes.text: Fleabag: 'Nowhere.' — her response to 'where'd you just go?'
jokes.setup: The Priest has noticed Fleabag's camera-break and asked where she went.
jokes.punchline: 'Nowhere.' — the lie, delivered simply. The audience knows exactly where she went (to them), and 'nowhere' is simultaneously the only possible answer and an obvious untruth. The comedy and the tragedy fuse.
jokes.explanation: The button on joke #71. One word, but it's the right word. 'Nowhere' is what you say when you've been somewhere private, somewhere no one else can see. In the context of the series, it's Fleabag acknowledging and denying her inner life simultaneously. The comedy is in the audience's knowledge: we know exactly where she went. It's a perfect, sad, funny lie.

tmdb_overview: Fleabag helps with an event at Claire's work which inevitably ends in disaster, but an introduction to Claire's colleague proves intriguing. A chance meeting with a stranger opens her eyes.
